title: Stacks & Queues
description: Linear data structures: Stacks (LIFO) and Queues (FIFO). Implementation strategies using lists, collections.deque, and their respective performance metrics.

sections.title: Stacks (LIFO)
sections.description: A Stack is a Last-In-First-Out data structure. It is easily simulated using Python's standard list, using append() to push and pop() to remove the last element in O(1) time.
sections.codeBlock:
stack = []

# Push elements
stack.append("Page A")
stack.append("Page B")
stack.append("Page C")

# Pop elements
print(stack.pop())     # "Page C"
print(stack.pop())     # "Page B"
print(stack)           # ["Page A"]

sections.title: Queues (FIFO)
sections.description: A Queue is a First-In-First-Out data structure. Implementing a queue using list.pop(0) is inefficient because it shifts all remaining elements in memory. Instead, use collections.deque for O(1) pops.
sections.codeBlock:
from collections import deque
queue = deque()

# Enqueue elements
queue.append("Request 1")
queue.append("Request 2")
queue.append("Request 3")

# Dequeue elements
print(queue.popleft())  # "Request 1" (O(1) pop operation)
print(queue.popleft())  # "Request 2"
print(list(queue))     # ["Request 3"]

details:
Stack Complexity: Stacks are fundamental to backtracking algorithms and recursion simulation. Since Python lists are dynamic arrays, appending and popping from the end are amortized O(1) operations, making lists highly efficient stack structures. If fixed size is required, use collections.deque with the optional maxlen argument.

Queue Gotchas: A naive implementation of a queue using a list (e.g. list.pop(0)) causes a massive performance bottleneck. Because lists are contiguous arrays, removing the first element requires the interpreter to shift the remaining N-1 elements down in memory, resulting in O(N) complexity. In contrast, collections.deque (double-ended queue) is structured internally as a doubly-linked list of fixed-size blocks. It guarantees O(1) appends and pops on both ends, which is optimal for network packet buffering, scheduling queues, and sliding windows.
